title-group.article-title: Hypochlorous acid via peroxynitrite activates protein kinase Cθ and insulin resistance in adipocytes

contrib-group.aff: Section of Molecular Medicine, Department of Internal Medicine, University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Oklahoma City, OK 73104, USA

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence: Ming-Hui Zou, MD, PhD, BSEB 306A, Section of Molecular Medicine, Department of Internal Medicine, University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Oklahoma City, OK 73104, Tel: 405-271-3974, Fax: 405-271-3973, ming-hui-zou@ouhsc.edu

fn.p: These authors contribute equally to this work.

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

related-article.article-title: RETRACTION: Hypochlorous acid via peroxynitrite activates protein kinase Cθ and insulin resistance in adipocytes

abstract.p: We recently reported genetically deletion of myeloperoxidase (MPO) alleviates obesity-related insulin resistance in mice in vivo. How MPO impairs insulin sensitivity in adipocytes is poorly characterized. Since hypochlorous acid (HOCl) is a principle oxidant from MPO, we evaluated the effects of HOCl on insulin signaling in adipocytes differentiated from 3T3-L1 cells. Exposure of 3T3-L1 adipocytes to exogenous HOCl (200 µmol/L) attenuated insulin-stimulated 2-deoxyglucose uptake, GLUT4 translocation, and insulin signals, including tyrosine phosphorylation of insulin receptor substrate-1 (IRS-1) and phosphorylation of Akt. Further, HOCl treatment induced phosphorylation of IRS-1 at Serine 307, inhibitor κB kinase (IKK), c-Jun NH2-terminal kinase (JNK), and phosphorylation of PKCθ (PKCθ). In addition, genetic and pharmacological inhibition of IKK and JNK abolished serine phosphorylation of IRS1 and impairment of insulin signaling by HOCl. Furthermore, PKCθ knockdown using siRNA transfection suppressed phosphorylation of IKK and JNK, and consequently attenuated HOCl-impaired insulin signaling pathway. Moreover, activation of PKCθ by peroxynitrite was accompanied by increased phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1-serine 307. In contrast, ONOO− inhibitors abolished HOCl-induced phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK and IRS1-serine 307, as well as insulin resistance. Finally, high fat diet-induced insulin resistance was associated with enhanced phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK, and IRS1 at serine 307 in white adipose tissues from wild type mice, all of which were not found in MPO knockout mice fed high fat diets. We conclude that HOCl impairs insulin signaling pathway by increasing ONOO−-mediated PKCθ phosphorylation, resulting in IKK/JNK phosphorylation and consequent serine phosphorylation of IRS1 in adipocytes.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Insulin resistance, a hallmark of obesity and fundamental cause of type 2 diabetes, is characterized by a diminished ability of insulin to regulate glucose homeostasis in insulin sensitive organs including liver, skeletal muscle and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance is partly caused by chronic low-level inflammation and oxidative stress in adipose tissue (Guo 2014). Infiltration of inflammatory cells, which product cytokines and oxidants, leads to a local inflammatory environment in adipose tissue (Olefsky and Glass 2010). Accumulated evidences suggest that increased neutrophils infiltration in adipose tissue is strongly related to the development of insulin resistance in human obesity (Elgazar-Carmon, et al. 2008; Talukdar, et al. 2012). Recently, we reported that neutrophil-derived myeloperoxidase (MPO) activation play an important role in obesity-induced insulin resistance (Wang, et al. 2014). Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) is a potent oxidant formed from hydrogen peroxide and chloride ions in a reaction catalyzed by MPO (Harrison and Schultz 1976). Recently, high correlation between HOCl production with metabolic disorder was identified, i.e. HOCl concentration in plasma was elevated in the obese subjects and hypertension patients (Yang, et al. 2013). In addition, HOCl-modified proteins were present in liver and adipose tissue of obese patients (Rensen, et al. 2009). However, molecular mechanism and role of HOCl on the pathogenesis of insulin resistance remains to be determined.

Insulin signaling transduction cascade initiated by insulin binding to its receptor causing receptor autophosphorylation and tyrosine phosphorylation of insulin receptor substrate-1 (IRS-1), subsequently activated phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K) and Akt, finally induced intracellular GLUT4 vesicles translocation to cell membrane to enhance glucose uptake (Bevan 2001). And insulin resistant is caused by impaired insulin signal transduction accompany with decreased activation of downstream obligate molecular intermediates (Saltiel and Kahn 2001). Accumulating evidences indicated that serine phosphorylation of IRS1 led to inhibition of insulin signals via interference of tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 and acceleration of its degradation (Gual, et al. 2005). Moreover, several inflammatory kinases activation, including inhibitor κB kinase (IKK), c-Jun NH2-terminal kinase (JNK) and protein kinase C (PKC), induced serine phosphorylation of IRS1 by inflammatory cytokines and pro-oxidants (Aguirre, et al. 2000; Morino, et al. 2005; Weigert, et al. 2008). HOCl is known as a potent oxidant and major inflammatory mediator to induce tissue injury in a number of inflammatory diseases (Souza, et al. 2011). Therefore, we hypothesize that HOCl mediates insulin resistance through activation of PKC, IKK, and JNK, resulting in a subsequent serine phosphorylation of IRS1 in adipocytes. Here, we report that exogenous HOCl impaired insulin signaling pathway and induced phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307, IKK, JNK and PKCθ in 3T3-L1 adipocyte. In contrast, HOCl-impaired insulin signals were abolished after IKKβ and JNK knockdown using siRNA or pharmaceutical inhibitor. Moreover, PKCθ knockdown attenuated phosphorylation of IKK and JNK, resulting in restoration of insulin sensitivity. Strikingly, exogenous HOCl-induced insulin resistance and phosphorylation of PKCθ were prevented by ONOO− scavenger. Overall, our findings provide a novel mechanistic basis for understanding how MPO-derived HOCl mediates insulin resistance in adipocytes.

sec.title: Materials
sec.p: Mouse 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, VA). 3T3-L1 preadipocyte medium, 3T3-L1 adipocyte differentiation medium, and 3T3-L1 adipocyte maintenance medium were obtained from Zen-Bio, Inc. (Research Triangle Park, NC). [1, 2-3H] 2-deoxy-D-glucose was purchased from Perkin Elmer Life Sciences (Waltham, MA). Hypochlorous sodium solution, human insulin, Nω-Nitro-L-arginine methyl ester hydrochloride (L-NAME), SP600125, and antibody against phospho-IRS-1 (Tyr612) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Protein A/G-agarose, RIPA lysis buffer, PS-1145, IKKβ siRNA, JNK2 siRNA, PKCθ siRNA, control siRNA, and antibodies against β-actin, GAPDH and Na+/K+ ATPase were obtained from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc. (Santa Cruz, CA). Antibodies against phospho-IRS-1 (Ser307), IRS-1, phospho-Akt (Ser473), phospho-Akt (Thr308), Akt, phospho-GSK3β (Ser9), GSK3β, phospho-IKKα/β (Ser176/180), IKKα, IKKβ, phospho-SAPK/JNK (Thr183/Tyr185), JNK, phospho-PKCθ (Thr538), PKCθ, GLUT4, IκBα, and horseradish peroxidase-linked secondary antibodies (Beverly, MA). ONOO− was obtained from Calbiochem (Billerica, MA). All other chemicals were of the highest commercial grade available.

sec.title: Cell Treatment
sec.p: 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were cultured and differentiated into adipocytes as previously described (Worrall and Olefsky 2002). By day 8–12 after differentiation induction, more than 90% of the cells exhibited the characteristic lipid vesicles-filled adipocyte phenotype. After overnight incubation in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium supplemented with 0.1% bovine serum albumin, 3T3-L1 adipocytes were exposed to various concentrations of HOCl in the presence of 0.1% BSA for 1 h. HOCl-treated 3T3-L1 adipocyte were further stimulated with insulin for 15 minutes. HOCl was prepared by acidizing hypochlorous sodium with hydrochloric acid to adjust the pH4.0 according to previous protocol (Wang, et al. 2007). The concentrations of HOCl were determined spectrophotometrically at 292 nm in 0.1 mol/L NaOH [ε = 350 (mol/L)−1· cm−1].

sec.title: 2-Deoxyglucose (2-DOG) Uptake
sec.p: The glucose uptake was assayed as described previously (Worrall and Olefsky 2002). Following overnight serum starvation, adipocytes were starved of glucose for 1h in HEPES buffer containing 0.1% BSA and exposed to different concentrations of HOCl for 1h. Then, cells were stimulated with 100 nM insulin for 15 min and following addition of 0.2 µCi [1, 2-3H] 2-DG for 5 min. Finally, cells were washed three times with ice-cold PBS buffer, and solubilized with 1% Triton X-100. 3H-2-DG uptake was determined using a liquid scintillation counter. The intracellular concentration of 2-DG was normalized to total protein content.

sec.title: Western Blotting Analysis
sec.p: Proteins were extracted from 3T3-L1 cells with RIPA lysis buffer (Santa Cruz) containing 1 mM Na3VO4, 1 µg/ml leupeptin, and 1 mM phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride. Protein concentration was measured using the BCA method (Pierce, Rockford, IL). Cell lysates were resolved by SDS-PAGE and transferred to PVDF membrane (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA). Membranes were blocked with 5% milk and probed with specific antibodies and subsequently incubated with horseradish peroxidase-linked secondary antibodies. Proteins were visualized by using enhanced chemiluminescence detection system (Pierce, Rockford, IL).

sec.title: siRNA Transfection
sec.p: IKKβ, JNK1, PKCθ and control siRNA (10 µmol/L) were added to OPTI-MEM reduced serum media (Life Technologies, Grand Island) with Lipofectamine RNAiMAX (Invitrogen Corp, Carlsbad). Adipocytes in 6-well plates were transfected with siRNA in transfection medium for 6 hrs. The transfection medium was then replaced with a culture medium containing 10% FBS and incubate for 48 h.

sec.title: Assay of in vitro Akt kinase activity
sec.p: A total of 500 µg protein was incubated with anti-Akt antibody and Sepharose beads overnight at 4°C. After binding, the beads were washed four times with lysis buffer. Akt activity was measured using non-radioactive Akt kinase assay kit (Cell signaling Technology, Inc, Beverly).

sec.title: Experimental animals
sec.p: MPO knockout (MPO−/−) mice was purchased from The Jackson Laboratory (Bar Harbor, ME). C57BL/6J mice were used as wild-type (WT) control. Mice were housed in temperature-controlled cages under a 12-h light/12-h dark cycle. Starting at 6 weeks of age, male mice were fed a high fat diet (HFD, D12492; Research Diets, New Brunswick) consisting of 60% fat, 20% protein, and 20% carbohydrate, or a normal chow diet (ND) consisting of 13% fat, 29% protein, and 58% carbohydrates (LabDiet, St. Louis) for up to 16 weeks. At the end of experiments, mice were fasted for 6 h. Body weight and food intake were measured. Blood samples were collectd for detecting fasting glucose and plasma insulin (ALPCO®, Salem, NH). Epididymal white adipose tissue (WAT) was collected and stored at −80°C. The animal protocol was reviewed and approved by the University of Oklahoma Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee.

sec.title: Immunohistochemical analysis
sec.p: Epididymal WAT from HFD-fed WT and MPO−/− mice were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 16 h, and embedded in paraffin. Sections were deparaffinized, rehydrated, and microwaved in citrate buffer for antigen retrieval. Sections were successively incubated in 3% hydrogen peroxide, protein block buffer, and primary antibodie against HOCl-oxidased low density lipoprotein (Millipore) overnight at 4 °C. Then, sections were rinsed in PBS buffer, and incubated with labeled polymer-horseradish peroxidase anti-rabbit antibody and DAB chromogen.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Values are expressed as mean ± SEM. One-way ANOVA was used to compare the differences among three groups followed by Bonferroni’s multiple comparison tests as applicable, and P<0.05 was considered significant.

sec.title: HOCl impairs insulin-stimulated glucose uptake, insulin signals and GLUT4 translocation
sec.p: We first investigate the effect of HOCl on insulin-stimulated glucose uptake measurement with 2-deoxyglucose labeled with tritium in 3T3-L1 adipocytes. Early study by our group and other lab (Ginion, et al. 2011; Liu, et al. 2013) on time-action and dose-response curve for insulin-stimulated glucose uptake revealed that 100 nmol/l insulin for 15 min stimulation could induce maximal insulin action, which dose was used for entire study to induce glucose uptake and insulin signaling transduction in 3T3-L1 adipocytes. HOCl dose-dependently decreased insulin-stimulated glucose uptake but not basal rate of glucose uptake, suggesting an insulin-resistant state. HOCl with pathologically relevant oxidant concentration (200 µmol/L) (Hawkins, et al. 2001) showed approximately 70% suppression of glucose uptake (Fig. 1A). Therefore, 200 µmol/L HOCl was used to treat adipocytes in the following experiments. Insulin could induce translocation of GLUT4 to cellular membrane, where increase uptake of glucose into cells. HOCl treatment inhibited insulin-stimulated GLUT4 translocation to the plasma membrane, while not affecting GLUT4 expression in the whole-cell lysates (Fig. 1B).

Next, we measured the molecular targets for HOCl-induced changes of glucose uptake. Insulin treatment stimulated phosphorylation of IRS1 at tyr612 (Fig. 2A), Akt and its downstream substrates GSK3β. Pretreatment of adipocytes with HOCl inhibited insulin-induced Akt and GSK3β phosphorylation (Fig. 1C). In parallel, HOCl treatment suppressed Akt kinase activity as evaluated by phosphorylation of GSK3β fusion protein (Fig. 1D). These results indicated that HOCl treatment induced insulin resistance in adipocytes.

sec.title: HOCl promotes phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307, IKK and JNK
sec.p: Recent studies reported that serine phosphorylation of IRS1, mediated by JNK and IKK, were associated with inhibition of insulin signaling pathway by inflammatory cytokines (Aguirre et al. 2000; Morino et al. 2005; Weigert et al. 2008). Thus, we examined the effect of HOCl on serine phosphorylation of IRS1 at 307 residues. As shown in Fig. 2A, HOCl treatment dramatically increased phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307 in the presence or absence of insulin, but not IRS1 expression. Meanwhile, insulin-stimulated IRS1 tyrosine phosphorylation was suppressed after HOCl pretreatment. In parallel, HOCl treatment dramatically increased phosphorylation of IKKα/β at Ser176/180 and JNK at Thr183/Tyr185, but not protein expression (Fig. 2B and 2C).

sec.title: IKKβ phosphorylation is involved in HOCl-induced insulin resistance
sec.p: Next, we investigated whether IKKα/β was required for HOCl-triggered phosphorylation of IRS1-Ser307 and insulin resistance. To this end, IKK-selective inhibitor PS-1145 (10 µmol/L) pretreated adipocytes before the addition of HOCl. PS-1145 suppressed HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IKKα/β and reduced serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 at 307. Further, PS-1145 significantly abrogated HOCl-impaired insulin signals, including tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS-1, phosphorylation of Akt and GSK-3β (Fig. 3A). To confirm this result, IKKβ-specific small interfering RNA (siRNA) was used to suppress IKK expression, and then evaluated HOCl-impaired insulin signals. IKKβ siRNA significantly decreased expression of IKKβ and HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IKKβ. Further, IKKβ siRNA attenuated serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 at 307, and restored phosphorylation of IRS-1 at Tyr612, phosphorylation of Akt and GSK-3β by insulin (Fig. 3B). These data provide strong evidences that IKKβ is required for serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 at 307 and impairment of insulin signaling after HOCl treatment.

sec.title: JNK phosphorylation is required for HOCl-induced insulin resistance
sec.p: We also determined whether JNK phosphorylation mediated phosphorylation of IRS1-Ser307 and insulin resistance after HOCl stimulation. As expected, JNK-specific inhibitor SP-600125 (30 µmol/L) markedly suppressed HOCl-induced JNK phosphorylation accompany with serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 at 307 residues. In parallel, SP-600125 significantly abrogated HOCl-induced impairment of insulin signals, as demonstrated by restoration of phosphorylation of IRS-1 at Tyr612, Akt and GSK-3β by insulin (Fig. 4A).

Next, we assayed whether genetic suppression of JNK altered HOCl-impaired insulin signaling pathway. As shown in Fig. 4B, JNK siRNA transfection significantly decreased JNK expression and HOCl-induced phosphorylation of JNK. And HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307 was dramatically decreased in JNK siRNA-transfected cells, compared to control siRNA-treated cells. Moreover, JNK siRNA abrogated HOCl-impaired insulin signaling, as demonstrated by restoration of insulin-stimulated tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS-1, phosphorylation of Akt and GSK-3β. These results indicate that JNK phosphorylation is required for insulin resistance and IRS-1 phosphorylation at Ser307 in 3T3-L1 adipocytes treatment with HOCl.

sec.title: PKCθ mediates HOCl-induced insulin resistance via IKK and JNK activation
sec.p: PKCθ could activate IKKβ and JNK, leading to phosphorylate of IRS-1 at Ser307 and Ser302 (Werner, et al. 2004). Thus, we verify whether PKCθ activation contributes to HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IKK and JNK and insulin resistance. Exposure of 3T3-L1 adipocytes to HOCl induces phosphorylation of PKCθ at Thr538. And PKCθ knockdown by siRNA transfection partly attenuated HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IKK and JNK, suggesting that HOCl activates IKK and JNK in a PKCθ-dependent manner (Fig. 5A). In parallel, PKCθ siRNA transfection dramatically decreased HOCl-induced serine phosphorylation of IRS1 at 307 and restored insulin-stimulated tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS-1 (Fig. 5B). Moreover, PKCθ siRNA partially recovered insulin-enhanced Akt kinase activity (Fig. 5C) and GLUT4 translocation to the plasma membrane (Fig. 5D). Taken together, these results suggest that PKCθ phosphorylation is involved in HOCl-induced insulin resistance via activation of IKK and JNK in 3T3-L1 adipocytes.

sec.title: ONOO− mediates phosphorylation of PKCθ and IRS1-Ser307 after HOCl treatment
sec.p: Our lab demonstrated that HOCl enhanced ONOO− production, a potent oxidative molecular formed by superoxide (O2·−) and nitric oxide (NO) (Wang et al. 2014). And ONOO− plays a causal role in the pathogenesis of insulin resistance in obese and type 2 diabetes (Randriamboavonjy and Fleming 2009). To determine whether ONOO− was involved in HOCl-induced inflammatory kinases activation and insulin resistance, 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pre-incubated with L-NAME (1 mmol/L) to inhibit NO production, Cu/Zn SOD (SOD1, 150 U/mL) to remove O2·−, or uric acid (50 µmol/L) to scavenge ONOO− before HOCl stimulation, and then phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK, and IRS1 were determined. As shown in Fig. 6A, HOCl-induced phosphorylation of PKCθ was blocked by SOD1, L-NAME and uric acid treatment. In addition, ONOO− dose-dependently induced phosphorylation of PKCθ, but not protein expression (Fig. 6B), suggesting that ONOO− contributes to PKCθ phosphorylation by HOCl.

We further confirmed the role of ONOO− in PKCθ-mediated downstream signals, including IKK, JNK, and IRS1 serine phosphorylation. As depicted in Fig. 6C, SOD1, L-NAME and uric acid suppressed phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1-Ser307 in adipocytes treatment with HOCl. In addition, ONOO− dose-dependently induced phosphorylation of IKK and JNK. Meanwhile, ONOO− increased serine phosphorylation of IRS1 and reduced tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 by insulin (Fig. 6D). Collectively, these results indicate that ONOO− mediates HOCl-induced Ser307 phosphorylation of IRS-1 via activation of PKCθ, IKK, and JNK.

sec.title: HOCl-induced insulin resistance is ONOO−-dependent
sec.p: Pretreatment of SOD1, L-NAME, and uric acid all prevented HOCl-induced impairment of insulin signals, as demonstrated by restoration of tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS-1, phosphorylation of Akt, and Akt kinase activity by insulin (Fig. 7A–C). Moreover, SOD1, L-NAME, and uric acid abolished the deleterious effect of HOCl on GLUT4 translocation to plasma membrane (Fig. 7D). In contrast, hydrogen peroxidase scavenger catalase offered no protection against HOCl-induced impairment of insulin signaling. Taken together, these results indicate that ONOO− is involved in HOCl-induced insulin resistance.

sec.title: Deletion of MPO attenuates HFD-induced phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1 Ser307
sec.p: MPO is the sole mammalian oxidant enzyme to catalyzed production of HOCl in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and chloride ions (Olza, et al. 2012). To extend our in vitro findings, we measured the insulin signals and molecular pathway involved in insulin resistance in WT and MPO knockout (MPO−/−) mice. The initial body weight for WT and MPO−/− male mice at 6 weeks of age were similar. After 16 weeks of HFD feeding, WT mice displayed higher body weight, fasting blood glucose and plasma insulin level than MPO−/− mice. In parallel, high level of homeostasis model-assessment of insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) indicated that WT mice developed severe insulin resistance than MPO−/− mice (Table 1). In epididymis WAT of WT mice fed with HFD, neutrophils infiltration (Wang et al. 2014) and MPO expression were increased compared to ND-fed WT mice (Fig. 8A). In addition, 3-chlorotyrosine as HOCl biomarker was present in the WAT from WT mice, mainly in the crown-like structure that contains neutrophils and mascrophages. This 3-chlorotyrosine stain was absent in MPO−/− mice (Fig. 8B). In parallel, HFD increased phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307, PKCθ, JNK and IKK in WAT, while not affecting protein expression. However, the effect of HFD on phosphorylation of IRS1-Ser307, PKCθ, JNK and IKK was absent in MPO−/− mice (Fig. 8C and D).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The present study has unveiled the mechanism whereby HOCl induces insulin resistance in adipocytes. We show that clinically relevant concentration of HOCl impairs insulin-stimulated glucose uptake, reduces the amplitude of the insulin signal, and enhances phosphorylation of IRS1 at serine307. Most importantly, HOCl induces PKCθ-dependent activation of IKK/JNK by ONOO−, causing serine phosphorylation of IRS1 and insulin resistance (Fig. 8E).

The key evidences can be summarized as follows: First, HOCl induces phosphorylation of IKK and JNK, whereas inhibition of IKK or JNK blocks serine phosphorylation of IRS1 and impairment of insulin signals. These data suggest that both IKK and JNK activation by HOCl is required for insulin resistance; Second, HOCl induces phosphorylation of PKCθ, and suppression of PKCθ attenuates IKK/JNK phosphorylation and restores insulin-stimulated glucose uptake, implying that PKCθ serves as an upstream kinase of IKK/JNK; Third, ONOO− treatment dose-dependently induces phosphorylation of PKCθ, whereas ONOO− scavenger reduces HOCl-stimulated phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK, and IRS1-Ser307. These data indicates that ONOO− is the initial trigger for the development of insulin resistance by HOCl. Finally, MPO deletion protects against high fat diet-induced phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK and IRS1-Ser307 accompany with insulin resistance in WAT. Overall, our results suggest that ONOO− mediates PKCθ-dependent serine phosphorylation of IRS1 might be responsible for HOCl-induced insulin resistance.

IRS1 is an essential insulin signal transducer to active downstream signals after tyrosine phosphorylation. But serine/threonine phosphorylation of IRS1 exerts opposite regulatory effect on insulin signaling via accelerated degradation and interference with tyrosine phosphorylation. Several inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-1β, induce serine phosphorylation of IRS1 at 307 and insulin resistance. Interesting, mutation of serine307 to alanine (IRS1 S307A) causing dephosphorylation this site eliminates TNF-α-decreased tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 in Chinese hamster ovary cells, underlining the important inhibitory function of this site on insulin signaling transduction (Aguirre et al. 2000). Weigert et al confirmed that IRS1 S307A mutation enhanced Akt phosphorylation in skeletal muscle cells (Weigert et al. 2008). Consistently, muscle-specific IRS1-Ser302, 307, and 612 mutant increased glucose uptake and insulin signaling (Morino, et al. 2008). Thus, we hypothesized that inhibition of IRS1 tyrosine phosphorylation and impaired insulin signaling pathway might be due to serine phosphorylation of IRS1 by HOCl. Indeed, we found that HOCl treatment increased serine phosphorylation of IRS1 at 307 and reduced insulin-stimulated tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 in 3T3-L1 adipocytes. Interesting, these opposite effects of HOCl on serine/tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 were simultaneously reserved after blockage of serine/threonine protein kinases activation, including IKK, JNK and PKCθ. These data demonstrate significantly negatively association between serine phosphorylation at 307 and tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 in adipocytes. Consistent with the conclusion, serine phosphorylation of IRS1 also strongly correlates with impaired insulin signaling pathway in liver and muscle of diabetic mice and human (Dong, et al. 2008; Morino et al. 2005). Recently, this conception has been challenged that Copps established a global IRS1 S307A knock-in mice and observed insulin resistance in 5-month-old mice fed with chow diet, suggesting that IRS1-Ser307 has a positive contribution to maintain good insulin sensitivity (Copps, et al. 2010). However, direct evidence is lacking to support that phosphorylation of IRS1 Ser307 cause inhibition of tyrosine phosphorylation and insulin resistance in adipocytes treatment with HOCl. Further investigation is warranted.

The inflammatory kinases IKKβ and JNK serve as critical molecular links between obesity, metabolic inﬂammation, and insulin resistance. Activation of IKK and JNK could phosphorylate IRS1 on a number of serine residues, resulting in inhibition of insulin signaling. IL-1β activates JNK1 and subsequently phosphorylates IRS1 at Ser307 in adipocyte (He, et al. 2006). Another cytokine TNF-α also IKK-dependently increases IRS1-Ser307 phosphorylation in 3T3-L1 adipocytes (Nakamori, et al. 2006). HOCl treatment induces JNK phosphorylation in primary biliary cells (Salunga, et al. 2007) and activates NFκB in endothelial cells (Pullar, et al. 2002). Therefore, we hypothesize that HOCl induces IRS1 serine phosphorylate via activation of IKK and JNK. Indeed, 3T3-L1 adipocytes exposure to HOCl significantly increases JNK and IKK phosphorylation. In contrast, pharmaceutical inhibitors and siRNA-mediated knockdown of JNK and IKKβ prevent impairment of insulin signaling and phosphorylation of IRS1 Ser307, suggesting that HOCl-mediated insulin resistance is dependent on activation of JNK and IKK. This conclusion is further supported by our in vivo studies that MPO knockout reduces phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1-Ser307 in WAT, in parallel with protects from insulin resistance in HFD-fed obese mice.

PKCθ has been reported to induce phosphorylate of IRS-1 on Ser307 and Ser302 via activation of IKKβ and JNK (Gao, et al. 2004). Free fatty acid is an inducer of PKCθ phosphorylation to cause development of insulin resistance in adipocytes (Gao et al. 2004) and skeletal muscle cells (Kadotani, et al. 2009). In present study, we demonstrate HOCl as a novel mediator for PKCθ activation in adipocytes, which might contribute to adipose inflammation and insulin resistance. HOCl treatment induces PKCθ phosphorylation in 3T3-L1 adipocyte. Moreover, PKCθ knockdown using siRNA transfection attenuates phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1-Ser307, and restored impairment of insulin signaling pathway by HOCl. These data implies PKCθ function as upstream of IKK and JNK to induce insulin resistance. It is noteworthy that PKCθ knockdown could not fully inhibit HOCl-induced activation of IKK and JNK, suggesting that HOCl treatment may activate IKK and JNK in other ways independent of PKCθ. Besides PKCθ, PKCζ are also involved in development of insulin resistance (Lee, et al. 2010). Our lab also reported that HOCl could induce phosphorylation of PKCζ, causing NADPH oxidase activation in endothelial cells (Xu, et al. 2006). Whether other PKC isoforms are involved in these processes require additional investigation.

We have recently reported that exogenous HOCl treatment increased ONOO− production in 3T3-L1 adipocytes and endothelial cells (Wang et al. 2014; Xu et al. 2006). ONOO− plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of insulin resistance through multiple pathways. For instance, ONOO− induces tyrosine nitration of insulin signaling proteins, including insulin receptor β and IRS1, leading to inactivation and degradation in adipocytes (Nomiyama, et al. 2004). ONOO− induces S-glutathiolation of p21ras and serine phosphorylation of IRS1 in endothelial cells as well (Clavreul, et al. 2006). In present study, we describe a novel signaling transduction mechanism by which HOCl-mediated insulin resistance is ONOO− dependent. ONOO− scavenger uric acid offers considerable protection against HOCl-induced phosphorylation of PKCθ and IRS1-Ser307. Because ONOO− is formed by the rapid reaction of NO with O2·−, O2·− and NO inhibitors show similar protective effect on phosphorylation of inflammatory kinases. On the other hand, ONOO− treatment directly induces phosphorylation of PKCθ, leading to reduction of tyrosine phosphorylation of IRS1 by insulin. These observations indicate that ONOO− is essential for inflammatory kinases activation, which triggers insulin resistance after HOCl stimulation.

In conclusion, the current findings strongly indicate that HOCl is a novel contributor to the development of insulin resistance in adipocytes, and clinically relevant concentration of HOCl induces ONOO− production and inflammatory kinases activation, resulting in impairment of insulin signaling pathway. HOCl-induced insulin resistance might represent a common pathological pathway in the development of the metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes.

fn.p: No potential conflicts of interest relevant to this article were reported.

Z.J. and Q.W. contributed equally to the study design, performed experiments, and wrote the manuscript. Y.D. performed some experiments. M.-H.Z. contributed to the study design and interpretation and wrote the manuscript. M.-H.Z. is the guarantor of this work and, as such, had full access to all the data in the study and takes responsibility for the integrity of the data and the accuracy of the data analysis.

ack.p: This work was supported by NIH grants (HL079584, HL080499, HL074399, HL089920, HL096032, HL10488, HL105157, and AG047776), a research award from the American Diabetes Association, and funds from Warren Chair in Diabetic Research from the University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center. Dr. Zou is a recipient of the National Established Investigator Award of the American Heart Association. Q.W. is a recipient of an AHA Postdoctoral fellowship.

element-citation.article-title: Positive and negative regulation of insulin signaling through IRS-1 phosphorylation
Insulin signaling, resistance, and the metabolic syndrome: insights from mouse models into disease mechanisms

Sin1 phosphorylation impairs mTORC2 complex integrity and inhibits downstream Akt signalling to suppress tumorigenesis

Reduction of insulin-stimulated glucose uptake by peroxynitrite is concurrent with tyrosine nitration of insulin receptor substrate-1

Oxidative stress-induced apoptosis of bile duct cells in primary biliary cirrhosis
Hypochlorous acid-induced heme degradation from lactoperoxidase as a novel mechanism of free iron release and tissue injury in inflammatory diseases
Neutrophils mediate insulin resistance in mice fed a high-fat diet through secreted elastase
Hypochlorous acid as a potential wound care agent: part I. Stabilized hypochlorous acid: a component of the inorganic armamentarium of innate immunity
Myeloperoxidase Deletion Prevents High-Fat Diet-induced Obesity and Insulin Resistance
Interplay and effects of temporal changes in the phosphorylation state of serine-302, −307, and −318 of insulin receptor substrate-1 on insulin action in skeletal muscle cells
Insulin resistance due to phosphorylation of insulin receptor substrate-1 at serine 302
The effects of intracellular calcium depletion on insulin signaling in 3T3-L1 adipocytes
Uncoupling of endothelial nitric oxidase synthase by hypochlorous acid: role of NAD(P)H oxidase-derived superoxide and peroxynitrite
Vascular VPO1 expression is related to the endothelial dysfunction in spontaneously hypertensive rats

caption.title: HOCl induces insulin resistance in 3T3-L1 adipocytes
caption.p: A, HOCl dose-dependently inhibits insulin-stimulated glucose uptake. Serum-starved 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pretreated with various concentrations of HOCl (0, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000 µmol/L) for 1 hour and stimulated with (▪) or without (●) 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min, and then incubated with [3H]-2-deoxyglucose (2-DOG) for 5 min. Glucose uptake was evaluated from [3H]-2-deoxyglucose counts in each cell lysate. Results are expressed as means ± S.E.M (n=5, *P<0.05, **P<0.01 vs. no HOCl treatment). B-D, HOCl impairs insulin signaling pathway. 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pretreated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Membrane proteins were isolated using the Mammalian Membrane Protein Extraction kit (Abcam). Cell lysis were subjected to SDS-PAGE and immunoblotted with indicated antibodies (B,C). Cell lysis was immunoprecipitated using Akt antibody and immunoblotted with phos-GSK3b for In vitro Akt kinase assay using non-radioactive Akt kinase assay kit (Cell signaling Technology) (D). The blot is a representative of blots obtained from five independent experiments.

caption.title: HOCl promotes phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307 association with JNK and IKKα/β
caption.p: 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pretreated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour before treatment with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min, and expression levels of phos-IRS1-Ser307 and phos-IRS-Tyr612 (A), IKK (B), and JNK (C) were determined by western analysis. The blot is a representative of blots obtained from five independent experiments.

caption.title: Inhibition of IKK blocks HOCl-induced serine phosphorylation of IRS1 and insulin resistance
caption.p: A. 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pretreated with 10 µmol/L PS1145 for 90 min, and incubated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and then stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. B. 3T3-L1 adipocytes were transfected with IKKβ siRNA or control siRNA for 48 hours and treated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and then stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Western analysis for protein expression and phosphorylation of IKK, IRS1, Akt and GSK3β. Blots are representative of the results from five independent experiments.

caption.title: Inhibition of JNK prevents HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IRS1 at Ser307 and insulin resistance
caption.p: A. 3T3-L1 adipocytes were pretreated with 30 µmol/L SP600125 for 90 min and incubated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and then stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. B. 3T3-L1 adipocytes were transfection with JNK2 siRNA or control siRNA for 48 hrs and treated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and then stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Western analysis for protein expression and phosphorylation of JNK, IRS1, Akt and GSK3β. Blots are representative of the results from five independent experiments.

caption.title: PKCθ mediates phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1 and insulin resistance
caption.p: 3T3-L1 adipocytes were transfected with PKCθ siRNA or control siRNA for 48 hours, and treated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour, and subsequently stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Western blot analysis of protein expression and phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK, IRS1(A–B). Akt kinase activity in cell lysis was measured (C). Membrane fraction was isolated and protein was subjected to SDS-PAGE and immunoblotted with GTLU4 antibody (D). The blot is a representative of blots obtained from five independent experiments.

caption.title: HOCl mediates inflammatory kinases phosphorylation through ONOO−
caption.p: A,C, 3T3-L1 adipocytes were serum-starved overnight, then treated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour in the presence of SOD1 (150 U/mL), L-NAME (1 mmol/L) or uric acid (UA, 50 µmol/L), followed by stimulation with 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Cell extracts were immunoblotted with indicated antibodies. B,D, 3T3-L1 adipocytes were serum-starved overnight, then treated with ONOO− (50, 100, 200 µmol/L) for 1 hour. Western blot analysis of protein expression and phosphorylation of PKCθ, IKK, JNK, and IRS1. The blot is a representative of blots obtained from five independent experiments.

caption.title: HOCl-induced insulin resistance is ONOO−-dependent
caption.p: 3T3-L1 adipocytes were serum-starved overnight, and treated with 200 µmol/L HOCl for 1 hour in the presence of SOD1 (150 U/mL), catalase (100 U/mL), L-NAME (1 mmol/L) or uric acid (50 µmol/L), then stimulated with or without 100 nmol/L insulin for 15 min. Cell extracts were immunoblotted with antibodies against IRS1 and Akt (A,B). Akt kinase activity in cell lysis was measured (C). Membrane proteins were isolated, and then subjected to SDS-PAGE and immunoblotted with GTLU4 antibody (D). The blot is a representative of blots obtained from five independent experiments.

caption.title: MPO deficiency prevents phosphorylation of IKK, JNK and IRS1-Ser307 in WAT from HFD-fed mice
caption.p: A,C,D, WT and MPO−/− mice were fed with ND or HFD for 16 weeks. Homogenates of epididymal WAT were prepared and levels of MPO, phosphorylation of IRS1, PKCθ, IKK and JNK were analyzed by western blot. B, Expression of 3-chlorotyrosine in WAT was analyzed by using immunohistochemistry. 20X. E, Schematic description for HOCl-induced phosphorylation of IRS at Ser307 and insulin resistance.
Body weight and metabolic characteristics in WT mice and MPO−/− mice after 16 weeks of HFD

fn.p: Values are mean ± S.E.M. N = 7 for each group.
P < 0.05. 6 weeks male mice were fed a high-fat diet for 16 weeks. Blood was collected after 6 h fasting and metabolic parameters were measured from serum.
